old_claim.note: Mechanical Refrigeration provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The blood-bank scope depends on preserving and storing donor blood, and the transfusion timeline identifies refrigerated storage as the storage method.

source_shape.source_locator: AABB timeline: Richard Weil demonstrated refrigerated storage of anticoagulated blood in 1915, and Fantus's 1937 hospital blood bank preserved and stored donor blood.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: AABB links stored blood to refrigerated storage before the first United States hospital blood bank and describes Fantus's bank as preserving and storing donor blood.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: For a node defined around stored donor blood, refrigeration is the direct preservation method that makes blood banking distinct from direct vein-to-vein transfusion.

ontology_before: Clinical blood banking treated mechanical refrigeration as a generic enabling capability.
ontology_after: Clinical blood banking requires refrigerated storage because preserved donor blood is central to the blood-bank workflow.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: clinical_blood_banking -> refrigeration is now a required edge.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to direct transfusion without stored donor blood.
A stronger source shows the scoped 1937 hospital blood bank did not depend on refrigerated storage.
The refrigeration node is narrowed away from artificial cooling used for medical storage.
short_reason: AABB identifies refrigerated storage as the preservation method behind stored donor blood.
